growth_records.cite: Madin JS et al. A synthesis of bacterial and archaeal phenotypic trait data. Sci Data 7:170 (2020); source dataset: engqvist.
growth_records.notes: Species-level trait record; culture mode/pH often unspecified; medium + uptake/secretion pending literature curation.

growth_records.strain: ISORED-2 (isoprene-reducing enrichment culture, homoacetogen dominating at 89-100% relative abundance; MAG ISORED-2)
growth_records.medium: Anaerobic bicarbonate-buffered mineral medium (H2/HCO3- fed) with isoprene; cell-suspension microcosm

up.units: umol/h (per microcosm; used as terminal electron acceptor)
up.method: reported/GC; value cited from the group's prior enrichment study (ref 23, Kronen et al. 2019 ISME J 13:1168-1182, doi:10.1038/s41396-018-0338-z)
electron donor for acetogenesis; no numeric rate reported in this paper
carbon source/electron acceptor for acetogenesis; no numeric rate reported

sec.units: relative (40% less acetate produced with isoprene than with H2+HCO3- alone)
sec.method: reported qualitatively (no absolute rate); consistent with isoprene reduction diverting reducing equivalents from acetogenesis
reduced product of isoprene hydrogenation; no absolute production rate given in this paper (only isoprene consumption rate)

product_yields.acetate: 40% lower with isoprene vs H2/HCO3- alone (qualitative)

growth_records.cite: Kronen, Miriam et al. Evidence for a Putative Isoprene Reductase in Acetobacterium wieringae. mSystems (2023). DOI:10.1128/msystems.00119-23. [PMC10134865]
growth_records.method: not reported (proteogenomic study; growth not quantified as mu)
growth_records.snippet: The homoacetogenic Acetobacterium lineage dominating the H2-fed enrichment used 1.6 umol isoprene h-1 as an electron acceptor in addition to HCO3-. Growth of the homoacetogen with isoprene produces 40% less acetate than with H2 and HCO3- al
growth_records.notes: Prokaryote = Acetobacterium wieringae (Gram-positive homoacetogen). This is a proteogenomics/comparative-genomics paper; no growth rate (mu), doubling time, or per-gDW metabolic rates are reported for the culture. Only quantitative physiology datum in-text is the isoprene consumption rate 1.6 umol/h, which is attributed to the H2-fed enrichment characterized in the group's PRIOR study (ref 23: Kronen et al. 2019, ISME J, doi:10.1038/s41396-018-0338-z), not newly measured here. Acetate 40% reduction is stated qualitatively (no absolute rate/yield). Cell-suspension conditions (30C, N2/5e4 Pa H2, 40 mM HCO3-, 1 mM isoprene) are from Fig. S1 legend. No arithmetic performed (no derivable numbers).

growth_records.medium: Anaerobic medium (isoprene as electron acceptor); composition not stated in this paper (see cited source)

up.method: reported; value cited from ref 24 (Jin et al. 2022, mBio 13:e02086-22, doi:10.1128/mbio.02086-22)

sec.method: predominant reduced product of isoprene (cometabolic, not linked to energy conservation per Jin et al.); no absolute production rate in this paper
product of 1,3-butadiene reduction by strain Y (qualitative, from cited source)

growth_records.cite: Kronen, Miriam et al. Evidence for a Putative Isoprene Reductase in Acetobacterium wieringae. mSystems (2023). DOI:10.1128/msystems.00119-23. [PMC10134865]
growth_records.snippet: Strain Y also transformed isoprene to predominately 2-methyl-1-butene with a similar rate of 1.74 umol isoprene h-1 (262.3 +/- 21.2 uM day-1). However, Jin et al. suggest that isoprene hydrogenation in strain Y is a cometabolic process and
growth_records.notes: Rate is SECONDHAND: measured on Acetobacterium wieringae strain Y in an independent study (ref 24: Jin et al. 2022, mBio, doi:10.1128/mbio.02086-22), merely quoted in this paper's introduction. Reported directly as 1.74 umol/h and 262.3 +/- 21.2 uM/day; no unit conversion or arithmetic needed. No growth rate, biomass, medium composition, temperature or pH given in this paper. Included for completeness and flagged as cited, not originally measured here.

growth_records.medium: Phosphate-buffered acetogen medium (30 mM, pH 7), CO as sole substrate; chemostat amended with 0.005% Antifoam 204

up.method: mass balance from gas partial pressures and flow rates (GC-TCD)

sec.method: steady-state mass balance (HPLC), quantitative GEM validation Table 2
steady-state mass balance (HPLC); essentially zero / slight net consumption

yields.product_yields: Acetate was the exclusive product in monoculture; CO consumption ~46% of inflow

growth_records.cite: Parera Olm et al. Synthetic Microbial Cocultivation for Targeted Production of Odd-Chain Carboxylates and Alcohols from Carbon Monoxide. Environmental Science & Technology (2025). DOI:10.1021/acs.est.4c14794. [PMC12424164]
growth_records.method: reported (D set at steady state; HRT 48 h -> D=0.021 h-1; mu=D at steady state)
growth_records.snippet: In monoculture, A. wieringae JM exclusively produced acetate (63.2 +/- 4.6 mM in R1 and 58.7 +/- 4.2 mM in R2). ... CO uptake rate (3.3 +/- 0.2 mmol h-1) ... model-predicted acetate production rate was significantly higher (0.65) than the o
growth_records.notes: Acetogen monoculture, reductive acetyl-CoA (Wood-Ljungdahl) pathway. Experimental values from Table 2 (quantitative GEM validation) and text. CO uptake 3.3+/-0.2 mmol/h; acetate 0.51+/-0.01 mmol/h; ethanol <-0.01 mmol/h. mu=D=0.021 h-1 (HRT 48 h). Doubling time = ln2/0.021 = 33.0 h. Per-gDW rates DERIVED using total biomass 0.32 g in 400 mL reactor (=0.8 g/L) stated as experimental in cFBA section: qCO=3.3/0.32=10.3 mmol/gDW/h; qAcetate=0.51/0.32=1.59 mmol/gDW/h (flagged approximate, is_experimental=false because 0.32 g is the community/model biomass value). CO consumption ~46% of ~430 mmol/L/d feed. Model (not experimental) predicted qCO 3.5+/-0.0 mmol/h and acetate 0.65+/-0.04. Qualitative growth (experimental) confirmed on CO, H2+CO2, formate, methanol, ethanol, glycerol, lactate, fructose, glucose (Table 2).

growth_records.strain: A. wieringae JM + C. kluyveri DSM 555
growth_records.medium: Phosphate-buffered acetogen medium (30 mM, pH 7) + 0.005% Antifoam 204, CO as substrate

up.method: mass balance; CO consumption rate unchanged from monoculture (Table S5)

yields.product_yields: Cell density increased only 5-10% vs monoculture; acetogen dominant

growth_records.cite: Parera Olm et al. Synthetic Microbial Cocultivation for Targeted Production of Odd-Chain Carboxylates and Alcohols from Carbon Monoxide. Environmental Science & Technology (2025). DOI:10.1021/acs.est.4c14794. [PMC12424164]
growth_records.snippet: Steady-state concentrations reached were 5.8 +/- 0.7 mM butyrate, 1.3 +/- 0.3 mM caproate and 41.7 +/- 4.5 mM acetate in R1 ... The CO consumption rate observed during cocultivation was the same as in the monoculture phase.
growth_records.notes: Coculture in CSTR (R1), even-chain elongation. C3-C6 carboxylic acids produced only from ethanol (electron donor) generated by A. wieringae JM from CO; ethanol not externally supplied. Steady-state concentrations reported (not per-cell rates); CO uptake rate stated identical to monoculture (~3.3 mmol/h, mass-transfer-limited). mu=D=0.021 h-1, td=33.0 h. Full steady-state rate table is Table S4/S5 (supplementary; not in main XML).

growth_records.strain: A. wieringae JM + A. neopropionicum DSM 3847
growth_records.medium: Phosphate-buffered acetogen medium (30 mM, pH 7) + 0.005% Antifoam 204, CO as substrate

yields.product_yields: Cell density +5-10% vs monoculture; A. neopropionicum makes propionate+acetate from acetogen-derived ethanol

growth_records.cite: Parera Olm et al. Synthetic Microbial Cocultivation for Targeted Production of Odd-Chain Carboxylates and Alcohols from Carbon Monoxide. Environmental Science & Technology (2025). DOI:10.1021/acs.est.4c14794. [PMC12424164]
growth_records.snippet: ... 2.4 +/- 0.5 mM propionate and 47.1 +/- 4.3 mM acetate in R2 ... propionate levels increased significantly following ethanol spiking.
growth_records.notes: Coculture in CSTR (R2). A. neopropionicum uses acrylate pathway on acetogen-derived ethanol to make propionate+acetate. Steady-state concentrations reported; CO uptake same as monoculture (mass-transfer-limited). mu=D=0.021 h-1, td=33.0 h.

growth_records.strain: A. wieringae JM + A. neopropionicum DSM 3847 + C. kluyveri DSM 555
growth_records.medium: MES-buffered bioreactor medium (pH 7); CO/N2/CO2 continuous gas feed + 20 mM ethanol initial

yields.product_yields: Total higher alcohols (C3-C6) 30.7 mM = 37.1 mol% of total products at 158 h; valerate 0.61 g/L (highest reported from CO/CO2); pentanol 0.33 g/L (highest reported from syngas/CO)

growth_records.cite: Parera Olm et al. Synthetic Microbial Cocultivation for Targeted Production of Odd-Chain Carboxylates and Alcohols from Carbon Monoxide. Environmental Science & Technology (2025). DOI:10.1021/acs.est.4c14794. [PMC12424164]
growth_records.method: not reported (OD600 max 3.3+/-0.5 at 135 h; final CDW 1.06+/-0.05 g/L; no mu computed)
growth_records.snippet: Final biomass concentration was 1.06 +/- 0.05 g L-1. CO consumption remained below 25% ... acetate was the main product (max. 27.0 +/- 3.3 mM) ... Butyrate (6.4 +/- 0.6 mM) and valerate (6.0 +/- 1.3 mM) ... Propanol ... (18.9 +/- 3.1 mM) ..
growth_records.notes: Synthetic triculture batch bioreactor, continuous CO feed. All values are endpoint/max soluble concentrations (mM), not per-cell rates (no biomass-normalized rates or mu reported). Final CDW 1.06 g/L; max OD600 3.3. CO consumption <25% of inflow. Estimated ~9 mM propionate + ~8 mM acetate from A. neopropionicum consuming the 20 mM ethanol supplied (author carbon-balance estimate). Products span even- and odd-chain MCCAs and C3-C6 alcohols. Some alcohol lost via gas stripping (detected in outflow bottle).

growth_records.medium: Phosphate-buffered acetogen medium (30 mM, pH 7), 100% CO headspace (170 kPa); serum-bottle batch, no carboxylate (control)

up.units: mmol total consumed over 9 days (per bottle)

yields.product_yields: Acetogenic control: CO fully consumed -> acetate + ethanol; final pH 4.8

growth_records.cite: Parera Olm et al. Synthetic Microbial Cocultivation for Targeted Production of Odd-Chain Carboxylates and Alcohols from Carbon Monoxide. Environmental Science & Technology (2025). DOI:10.1021/acs.est.4c14794. [PMC12424164]
growth_records.method: not reported (final cell density 0.52+/-0.06 g/L CDW at 9 days)
growth_records.snippet: none (control) 0.52 +/- 0.06 | 13.9 +/- 0.3 [100 +/- 0] | acetate 20.8 +/- 2.2 | ethanol 17.6 +/- 5.4 | final pH 4.8 (Table 1). Carbon/electron balances 91.7-101.4% / 85.4-114.1%.
growth_records.notes: Table 1 control row. Endpoint values after 9 days. CO consumed = 13.9 mmol total (100% of headspace CO). Crude average CO uptake ~ 13.9 mmol / (9 d) = 1.54 mmol/d per bottle; per biomass ~ 0.52 g/L x 0.05 L = 0.026 gDW -> ~2.5 mmol/gDW/h average (very approximate, biomass grew during incubation; not reported as specific rate). Final pH dropped to 4.8 due to acid accumulation.

growth_records.medium: Phosphate-buffered acetogen medium (30 mM, pH 7), 100% CO headspace (170 kPa), + 20 mM propionate; serum-bottle batch

up.units: mmol total consumed over 9 days (per bottle)

yields.product_yields: CO-driven reduction of propionate to propanol; 48% propionate consumed; final pH 5.1

growth_records.cite: Parera Olm et al. Synthetic Microbial Cocultivation for Targeted Production of Odd-Chain Carboxylates and Alcohols from Carbon Monoxide. Environmental Science & Technology (2025). DOI:10.1021/acs.est.4c14794. [PMC12424164]
growth_records.method: not reported (final cell density 0.49+/-0.10 g/L CDW at 9 days)
growth_records.notes: Table 1, +propionate row. A. wieringae JM reduces propionate to propanol using CO as electron donor; reduction uncoupled from growth. Endpoint (9 d): CO consumed 14.1 mmol (100%), propionate consumed 9.1 mM (48%), propanol 8.3 mM, acetate 31.1 mM, ethanol 11.0 mM, final pH 5.1.

growth_records.medium: Phosphate-buffered acetogen medium (30 mM, pH 7), 100% CO headspace (170 kPa), + 20 mM butyrate; serum-bottle batch

up.units: mmol total consumed over 9 days (per bottle)

yields.product_yields: CO-driven reduction of butyrate to butanol; 31% butyrate consumed; final pH 5.0

growth_records.cite: Parera Olm et al. Synthetic Microbial Cocultivation for Targeted Production of Odd-Chain Carboxylates and Alcohols from Carbon Monoxide. Environmental Science & Technology (2025). DOI:10.1021/acs.est.4c14794. [PMC12424164]
growth_records.method: not reported (final cell density 0.46+/-0.06 g/L CDW at 9 days)
growth_records.notes: Table 1, +butyrate row. Butyrate reduced to butanol with CO. Endpoint 9 d: CO 13.7 mmol (100%), butyrate consumed 4.9 mM (31%), butanol 4.4 mM, acetate 28.5 mM, ethanol 8.1 mM, pH 5.0.

growth_records.medium: Phosphate-buffered acetogen medium (30 mM, pH 7), 100% CO headspace (170 kPa), + 20 mM valerate; serum-bottle batch

up.units: mmol total consumed over 28 days (per bottle)

yields.product_yields: Sluggish CO-driven reduction of valerate to pentanol; only 25% valerate, 34% CO consumed; final pH 5.9

growth_records.cite: Parera Olm et al. Synthetic Microbial Cocultivation for Targeted Production of Odd-Chain Carboxylates and Alcohols from Carbon Monoxide. Environmental Science & Technology (2025). DOI:10.1021/acs.est.4c14794. [PMC12424164]
growth_records.method: not reported (final cell density 0.14+/-0.00 g/L CDW at 28 days; little biomass)
growth_records.notes: Table 1, +valerate row. Note 28-day incubation (vs 9 d for C3/C4). Low CO consumption (34%) and low biomass (0.14 g/L) = sluggish activity; valerate reduction to pentanol uncoupled from growth. Endpoint: CO 4.7 mmol (34%), valerate consumed 4.8 mM (25%), pentanol 4.0 mM, acetate 10.8 mM, ethanol 1.8 mM, pH 5.9.

growth_records.medium: Phosphate-buffered acetogen medium (30 mM, pH 7), 100% CO headspace (170 kPa), + 20 mM caproate; serum-bottle batch

up.units: mmol total consumed over 28 days (per bottle)

yields.product_yields: Very low CO-driven reduction of caproate to hexanol; only 10% caproate, 23% CO consumed; final pH 6.2 (caproate toxicity)

growth_records.cite: Parera Olm et al. Synthetic Microbial Cocultivation for Targeted Production of Odd-Chain Carboxylates and Alcohols from Carbon Monoxide. Environmental Science & Technology (2025). DOI:10.1021/acs.est.4c14794. [PMC12424164]
growth_records.method: not reported (final cell density 0.09+/-0.01 g/L CDW at 28 days; almost no biomass)
growth_records.notes: Table 1, +caproate row. 28-day incubation. Lowest activity (caproate toxicity): CO 3.2 mmol (23%), caproate consumed 1.7 mM (10%), hexanol 2.1 mM, acetate 9.8 mM, ethanol 0.5 mM, pH 6.2, biomass only 0.09 g/L. Reduction uncoupled from growth.
